# Cross-model performance & resource usage (wall-clock, GPU, memory, KV cache) — L1/L2/L3

**Date:** 2026-07-11
**Trigger:** Doc-walkthrough question — *"Did we log the performance of each model / how long each took? And is GPU, memory, KV-cache, etc. usage captured anywhere in one doc?"*
**Status:** Consolidated. This doc is the single source of truth for per-model, per-layer runtime and resource usage. Numbers are regenerated by `analysis/perf-resource-summary.py` → `results/summary/perf-resource-summary.csv`.

---

## Short answer

Yes — it was all captured, but until now it lived in three places and no single doc: the **ledgers** (`results/summary/l{1,2,3}-run-ledger.csv`, wall-clock + energy only), the per-run **`run-summary.json`** files (full GPU/memory/KV/throughput, but one file per run, never aggregated), and prose fragments in the report (§6 Measurement pitfalls, §7 Operational findings). This doc joins them.

**Two things to internalize before reading the table:**
1. **Telemetry asymmetry.** Inference-side metrics (decode tok/s, prefill tok/s, TTFT, KV-cache %) exist **only for the two vLLM models** (gpt-oss, qwen). nemotron runs on **TensorRT-LLM**, which exposes no Prometheus endpoint — so those cells are **blank by design, not missing data**. nemotron still has full hardware telemetry (GPU util/power/temp/clock, unified memory) and hardware energy.
2. **Wall-clock is runtime-confounded, not a clean speed rank.** nemotron's slowness mixes model verbosity **+** the TRT runtime **+** (at L3) parallel batching. Treat quality metrics as model-driven and comparable; treat throughput/energy/latency as **runtime-dependent** — the same disclosure rule as `infra/models.json` M17.

---

## Consolidated table

Wall-clock (`dur_*`) and energy come from the **ledgers** (complete — every run, including ones whose metric window failed). GPU/memory/KV/throughput come from **`run-summary.json`** (a subset — only runs whose collector window succeeded; `res_n` = how many). Gauges are the **median of per-run means**; unified memory is the **max per-run peak** (worst-case footprint).

| Layer | Model | runs (dur) | dur median | dur total | dur max | energy median | GPU util % | GPU power W | temp °C | SM MHz | peak unified mem (MiB / GiB) | KV cache % | decode tok/s | prefill tok/s | TTFT p50 s |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | gpt-oss-120b | 29 | 1.9 min | 1.31 h | 12.7 min | 3.6 kJ | 90.7 | 30.1 | 57 | 2393 | 113059 / 110.4 | 1.6 | 26.2 | 24218 | 0.466 |
| **L1** | qwen3-coder-30b | 29 | 1.4 min | 1.04 h | 7.5 min | 2.6 kJ | 89.3 | 29.2 | 56 | 2392 | 110455 / 107.9 | 4.5 | 20.1 | 16688 | 0.500 |
| **L1** | nemotron-super (TRT) | 21 | 3.9 min | 14.46 h | **778.9 min** | 8.4 kJ | 91.8 | 38.0 | 64 | 2404 | 108517 / 106.0 | — | — | — | — |
| **L2** | gpt-oss-120b | 27 | 11.1 min | 4.95 h | 20.1 min | 18.8 kJ | 88.5 | 30.8 | 60 | 2400 | 111132 / 108.5 | 2.2 | 31.0 | 53934 | 0.362 |
| **L2** | qwen3-coder-30b | 28 | 12.9 min | 6.25 h | 22.2 min | 21.5 kJ | 92.7 | 27.7 | 61 | 2398 | 110250 / 107.7 | 5.8 | 22.8 | 86220 | 0.206 |
| **L2** | nemotron-super (TRT) | 8 | 16.1 min | 2.18 h | 31.4 min | 40.6 kJ | 90.6 | 45.3 | 66 | 2427 | 107594 / 105.1 | — | — | — | — |
| **L3** | gpt-oss-120b | **0 ⚠** | — | — | — | — | 2.1 ⚠ | 12.6 ⚠ | 51 | 2409 | 116797 / 114.1 | 0.0 | — | — | — |
| **L3** | qwen3-coder-30b | 1 | 643 min | 10.72 h | 643 min | 1007 kJ | 95.2 | 26.1 | 62 | 2400 | 117762 / 115.0 | 0.6 | 24.2 | 1666 | 0.375 |
| **L3** | nemotron-super (TRT) | 1 | 547 min | **9.11 h ‡** | 547 min | 1479 kJ | 95.2 | 45.0 | 70 | 2464 | 117476 / 114.7 | — | — | — | — |

**⚠ gpt-oss L3 — data gap (not zero performance).** The ledger duration/energy are blank and the lone `run-summary.json` captured only 43 s at 2 % GPU util → the **metric collector window missed the actual generation run**. The pass@1 result (0.893) is unaffected — LiveCodeBench scoring is independent of the metrics window — but gpt-oss L3 has **no trustworthy wall-clock, energy, or throughput**. This is the one genuine metrics hole in the study; see "Known gaps."

**‡ nemotron L3 wall-clock is an 8-way parallel window.** 9.11 h is the batched wall-clock. Single-stream L3 for nemotron is **≈130 h** (bandwidth-bound verbose decode to the 8192-token budget; report §7). So the L3 duration column is **not** a single-stream speed comparison across models.

---

## What the numbers say

- **Per-task latency (L1, the clean comparison — same vLLM/TRT split aside).** qwen 1.4 min ≈ gpt-oss 1.9 min ≪ nemotron 3.9 min **median**. nemotron is ~**2–2.7× slower at the median**, but its **total** L1 time (14.46 h) is dominated by a single **778.9 min (≈13 h) runaway** run — this is *the* energy/time outlier the report keeps flagging. Always read **median, not mean/total**, for nemotron L1.
- **Energy scales with time, and nemotron draws more.** Median GPU power: nemotron ~38–45 W vs the vLLM pair ~27–31 W (nemotron's TRT config + longer decode). Combined with longer wall-clock, nemotron's per-task energy is 2–3× the vLLM models at every layer.
- **Decode is slow for everyone — memory-bandwidth-bound on GB10.** Decode throughput is ~20–31 tok/s across the vLLM models/layers (report §2). Prefill is 1–2 orders of magnitude faster (16k–86k tok/s), confirming decode, not prefill, is the wall.
- **Memory footprint is model-flat, workload-driven.** Peak unified memory sits ~106–115 GiB across the board (of 128 GiB). It's **higher at L3** (~114–115 GiB) than L1/L2 (~105–110 GiB) — the larger KV/batch of a 512-problem sweep — and is essentially the same across models because all three are ~similar-footprint served weights on one box.
- **KV-cache pressure was low the whole study.** vLLM KV-cache usage medians are **0.6–5.8 %** — the box was never KV-bound; single-/low-concurrency generation never filled the cache. (nemotron KV is unobservable on TRT; its TRT KV config is documented in `2026-06-30-kv-cache-quant-unified-memory.md` and M17.)

---

## Quality-adjusted efficiency (energy / time per *successful* task)

Raw tok/s is the wrong final comparison — the useful one is **cost per successfully completed task** (`llm_eval.txt` §10/final). We self-host, so **energy is the cost proxy**. Success = L1 SWE-bench `resolved`, L2 acceptance fraction ≥ 0.5 (`working_29`). Source: `analysis/quality-adjusted-efficiency.py` → `results/summary/quality-adjusted-efficiency.csv`.

| Layer | Model | success | success rate | tasks/hour | **energy / success** | GPU-min / success |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | gpt-oss-120b | 11/29 | 0.379 | 8.43 | **12.8 kJ** | 7.1 |
| L1 | qwen3-coder-30b | 7/29 | 0.241 | 6.74 | **15.4 kJ** | 8.9 |
| L1 | nemotron-super | 6/29 | 0.207 | 0.41 | **143.0 kJ** | 144.6 |
| L2 | gpt-oss-120b | 16/28 | 0.571 | 3.23 | **32.8 kJ** | 18.6 |
| L2 | qwen3-coder-30b | 3/28 | 0.107 | 0.48 | **211.4 kJ** | 125.0 |
| L2 | nemotron-super | 0/8 | 0.000 | — | — | — |

**This is the sharpest single result in the study.** nemotron and qwen solve *comparable* raw counts to gpt-oss at L1 (6–7 vs 11), but nemotron burns **~11× the energy per solved task** (143 vs 12.8 kJ) and **~20× the GPU-time** (144.6 vs 7.1 min) — its verbose, TRT-served, memory-bandwidth-bound decode makes each success far more expensive. gpt-oss is the efficiency winner at both layers. Caveats: energy/time are runtime-confounded (nemotron on TRT); "tasks/hour" is throughput at the concurrency each layer actually ran (≈seq=1), not a max-concurrency figure; L2 nemotron is floor-saturated (0 successes → undefined per-success cost).

## Tail latency (p50/p95) — TTFT and end-to-end

Added 2026-07-11 (`analysis/latency-tail-p95.py` → `results/summary/latency-tail-p95.csv`). p95 is the standard tail metric the taxonomy's minimum checklist asks for; we had only p50/p90/p99. **No re-run** — recomputed from the raw vLLM latency histograms already saved in every run's `vllm-metrics.csv`, reusing `aggregate.py`'s canonical `hist_quantiles` (so it's consistent with the published p50/p90/p99). Values below are the **median of per-run percentiles** (seconds). vLLM models only — nemotron (TRT) exposes no histograms; gpt-oss L3 is excluded (metrics-window failure, ⚠ above).

| Layer | Model | TTFT p50 | **TTFT p95** | e2e p50 | **e2e p95** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | gpt-oss-120b | 0.47 | **4.88** | 4.79 | **17.75** |
| L1 | qwen3-coder-30b | 0.50 | **3.88** | 5.00 | **19.88** |
| L2 | gpt-oss-120b | 0.36 | **1.60** | 6.50 | **36.33** |
| L2 | qwen3-coder-30b | 0.21 | **0.65** | 10.11 | **46.69** |
| L3 | qwen3-coder-30b | 0.38 | **0.49** | 52.63 | **238.56** |

TTFT stays sub-5 s at p95 even under the agentic layers; the long **e2e p95** tails (up to ~240 s at L3) reflect long single-shot generations, not first-token stalls — consistent with the memory-bandwidth-bound decode story.

## Regression rate (L1 — collateral damage on previously-passing tests)

Added 2026-07-11 (`analysis/regression-rate.py` → `results/summary/regression-rate.csv`). A **regression** = a patch that applied and ran but flipped ≥1 SWE-bench `PASS_TO_PASS` test to failing. **No re-run** — parsed from the retained per-instance eval reports (`logs/run_evaluation/<eval_run_id>/<model>/<instance>/report.json`). By SWE-bench's definition, `resolved` runs cannot regress, so this characterizes *failure quality* among **applied** patches, not the winners.

| Model | patches applied/eval | resolved | regressions | **regression rate** | PASS_TO_PASS tests broken |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| gpt-oss-120b | 24 | 11 | 8 | **0.333** | **1078** |
| qwen3-coder-30b | 22 | 7 | 7 | **0.318** | 95 |
| nemotron-super | 14 | 6 | 3 | **0.214** | 3 |

Reading it: **~1 in 3 applied gpt-oss/qwen patches broke something that was passing** (vs ~1 in 5 for nemotron). But the *severity* differs sharply — gpt-oss broke **1078** previously-passing tests vs nemotron's **3**: gpt-oss's aggressive, larger edits both **resolve the most** (11) *and* cause the most collateral damage (a handful of catastrophic patches — e.g. breaking an import so a whole module's suite fails), while nemotron's fewer, smaller edits rarely regress but rarely resolve. Denominators differ (24/22/14 applied) and n is the 29-task subset, so read this as directional. Per-instance detail in `results/summary/regression-rate-per-run.csv`.

## Provenance & method

- **Wall-clock + energy:** `results/summary/l{1,2,3}-run-ledger.csv` (`duration_s`, `energy_j`, `energy_valid`). Authoritative and complete — includes runs with no `run-summary.json`.
- **GPU / unified memory / KV / throughput / TTFT:** `results/raw/<run-id>/run-summary.json`, produced by `infra/metrics/` (three time-synced collectors: `collect-hw.sh` → `hw.csv`; `collect-vllm-prom.py` → `vllm-metrics.csv`; `collect-opencode.py`). See `infra/metrics/README.md`.
- **Aggregation:** `analysis/perf-resource-summary.py` → `results/summary/perf-resource-summary.csv`. Re-run any time: `python3 analysis/perf-resource-summary.py`.
- **GB10 measurement notes** (from `infra/metrics/README.md`): memory is unified so `nvidia-smi memory.*` is `[N/A]` — footprint comes from `/proc/meminfo`; power is **GPU-only** via `nvidia-smi` (DCGM/tegrastats not installed), so absolute Watts understate full-SoC draw but are consistent across models; `mem_controller_util_pct` reads 0 (DCGM-only signal absent) — decode-bound claims rest on throughput, not this counter.

## Known gaps (do not paper over)

1. **gpt-oss L3 metrics window failed** — no wall-clock/energy/throughput for that single run (⚠ above). Quality result stands. Recoverable only by re-running gpt-oss L3 under a correct metric window; not yet done.
2. **nemotron has no inference-side telemetry at any layer** (TRT exposes no Prometheus) — decode tok/s, prefill tok/s, TTFT, KV% are structurally unavailable, not lost.
3. **nemotron L1 resource metrics cover 21/29 runs** (8 runs lack `run-summary.json`); wall-clock/energy still complete via the ledger for the 21 valid-duration runs.
4. **L3 durations are not a controlled single-stream speed rank** — nemotron ran 8-way parallel; single-stream ≈130 h (‡ above).
